title: What3words

infobox.value: March 5, 2013; 13 years ago (2013-03-05) in [Royston, Hertfordshire](/wiki/Royston,_Hertfordshire "Royston, Hertfordshire"), England[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* Chris Sheldrick * Jack Waley-Cohen * Mohan Ganesalingam * Michael Dent

(stylized as **what3words**) is a [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software")[[4]](#cite_note-w3w-patent-4) [geocode system](/wiki/Geocode#Geocode_system "Geocode") designed to identify any location on the surface of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") with a resolution of approximately 3 metres (9.8 ft). It is owned by What3words Limited, based in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England. The system encodes [geographic coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinates "Geographic coordinates") into three permanently fixed dictionary words. For example, the front door of [10 Downing Street](/wiki/10_Downing_Street "10 Downing Street") in London is identified by `///slurs.this.shark`.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Proprietary geocoding system

What3words Limited

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [What3words Company Logo](/wiki/File:What3words_logo.png) | |
| Part of the What3Words grid on the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") showing typical words and their pseudorandom distribution | |
| [Trade name](/wiki/Trade_name "Trade name") | what3words |
| Founded | March 5, 2013; 13 years ago (2013-03-05) in [Royston, Hertfordshire](/wiki/Royston,_Hertfordshire "Royston, Hertfordshire"), England[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Founders | * Chris Sheldrick * Jack Waley-Cohen * Mohan Ganesalingam * Michael Dent |
| Headquarters | [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, UK[[2]](#cite_note-2) |
| Revenue | * Increase £2.2M [[3]](#cite_note-ch2024report-3) (2024) |
| [Operating income](/wiki/Earnings_before_interest_and_taxes "Earnings before interest and taxes") | * Increase −£14.1M [[3]](#cite_note-ch2024report-3) (2024) |
| [Net income](/wiki/Net_income "Net income") | * Increase −£10.6M [[3]](#cite_note-ch2024report-3) (2024) |
| Number of employees | 87[[3]](#cite_note-ch2024report-3) (2024) |
| Website | [what3words.com](https://what3words.com) |

**What3words** (stylized as **what3words**) is a [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software")[[4]](#cite_note-w3w-patent-4) [geocode system](/wiki/Geocode#Geocode_system "Geocode") designed to identify any location on the surface of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") with a resolution of approximately 3 metres (9.8 ft). It is owned by What3words Limited, based in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England. The system encodes [geographic coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinates "Geographic coordinates") into three permanently fixed dictionary words. For example, the front door of [10 Downing Street](/wiki/10_Downing_Street "10 Downing Street") in London is identified by `///slurs.this.shark`.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

What3words differs from most [location encoding systems](/wiki/Geodata "Geodata") in that it uses words rather than strings of numbers or letters, and the pattern of this mapping is not obvious; the algorithm mapping locations to words is copyrighted.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

What3words has been subject to a number of criticisms both for its closed source code[[7]](#cite_note-bbc2019-7) and the significant risk of ambiguity and confusion in its three word addresses.[[8]](#cite_note-arthurplos-8) This has led rescuers to advise against the use of What3words in safety critical applications.[[9]](#cite_note-Tierney-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Times20191226-10)

The company has a website, apps for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), and an [API](/wiki/API "API") for bidirectional conversion between What3words addresses and [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude")–[longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") coordinates.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Founded by Chris Sheldrick, Jack Waley-Cohen, Mohan Ganesalingam and Michael Dent, What3words was launched in July 2013.[[11]](#cite_note-w3w-launch-11)[[12]](#cite_note-w3w-launch-2-12) Sheldrick and Ganesalingam conceived the idea when Sheldrick, working as an event organizer, struggled to get bands and equipment to the appropriate [loading docks](/wiki/Loading_dock "Loading dock") and entrances of large music venues.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Sheldrick tried using GPS coordinates, but decided that words were better than numbers after transposing two digits led a driver to the wrong location. He credits a mathematician friend for the idea of dividing the world into 3-metre (10 ft) squares, and the linguist Jack Waley-Cohen with using memorable words.[[15]](#cite_note-FT16-15) The company was incorporated in March 2013[[16]](#cite_note-16) and a patent application for the core technology filed in April 2013.[[4]](#cite_note-w3w-patent-4) In November 2013, What3words raised [US$](/wiki/US$ "US$")500,000 of seed funding.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

What3words originally sold "OneWord" addresses, which were stored in a database for a yearly fee,[[12]](#cite_note-w3w-launch-2-12) but this offering was discontinued[[18]](#cite_note-18) as the company switched to a [business-to-business](/wiki/Business-to-business "Business-to-business") model.[[19]](#cite_note-bi2020-19) In 2015, the company was targeting logistics companies, post offices, and couriers.[[15]](#cite_note-FT16-15)

In January 2018, [Mercedes-Benz](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz "Mercedes-Benz") bought approximately 10% of the company and announced support for What3words in future versions of their [infotainment](/wiki/Infotainment "Infotainment") and navigation system.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Fear.Movie.Lions_IPA.jpg/250px-Fear.Movie.Lions_IPA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fear.Movie.Lions_IPA.jpg)

Stone Brewing Co. explains how customers can use What3Words to find anything... even beer.

In the summer of 2018, [Stone Brewing Co.](/wiki/Stone_Brewing_Co. "Stone Brewing Co.") produced the first What3words beer. Fear.Movie.Lions [IPA](/wiki/India_pale_ale "India pale ale"), designates the location of its [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia") brewery.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In March 2021, it was announced that [ITV plc](/wiki/ITV_plc "ITV plc") had invested £2 million in What3words as the first investment in its [media-for-equity](/wiki/Media_for_equity "Media for equity") scheme.[[23]](#cite_note-adw-23)

What3words has raised more than £50m from investors since launching.[[19]](#cite_note-bi2020-19) In 2024, what3words had a turnover of £2.2m and made a loss of £10.6m.[[3]](#cite_note-ch2024report-3)

## Source of revenue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Source of revenue")]

The what3words system and app is free for anyone to use. The company states that its revenue comes from charging businesses that benefit from its products.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Design")]

### Wordlists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Wordlists")]

What3words divides the world into a grid of 57 trillion 3-by-3-metre (10 ft × 10 ft) squares, each of which has a three-word address. The company says it does its best to remove [homophones](/wiki/Homophones "Homophones") and spelling variations;[[25]](#cite_note-25) however, at least 32 pairs of English near-homophones still remain.[[26]](#cite_note-cybergibbons-26)

Wordlists are available in 60 languages,[[27]](#cite_note-27) each of which uses a list of 25,000 words (except for English, which uses 40,000 to cover sea as well as land).[[28]](#cite_note-wired2018-28) Translations are not direct, as direct translations to some languages could produce more than three words. Rather, territories are localised "considering linguistic sensitivities and nuances".[[29]](#cite_note-FT21-29) Densely populated areas have strings of short words to aid more frequent usage; while less populated areas, such as the North Atlantic, use more complex words.[[29]](#cite_note-FT21-29)[[15]](#cite_note-FT16-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Northumberland_what3words_locations_%2C_extra.cowboy.manual_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7776828.jpg/250px-Northumberland_what3words_locations_%2C_extra.cowboy.manual_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7776828.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northumberland_what3words_locations_,_extra.cowboy.manual_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7776828.jpg)

Moseying to get to Cow Road in Spittal? Helps to have an extra.cowboy.manual

In a 2019 blog, [open standards](/wiki/Open_standards "Open standards") advocate and technology expert [Terence Eden](/wiki/Terence_Eden "Terence Eden") questioned the cultural neutrality of using words rather than the numbers generated by map coordinates. "Numbers are (mostly) culturally neutral." he said, "Words are not. Is mile.crazy.shade a respectful name for a war memorial? How about tribes.hurt.stumpy for a temple?"[[7]](#cite_note-bbc2019-7)

### Ambiguity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ambiguity")]

What3words state that similar addresses are spaced as far apart as possible to avoid confusion,[[30]](#cite_note-30) and that similarly sounding codes have a 1 in 2.5 million chance of pointing to locations near each other.[[31]](#cite_note-Wakefield-BBC-31)

However, security researcher Andrew Tierney calculates that 75% of What3words addresses contain plural words that also exist in singular form (or the reverse).[[26]](#cite_note-cybergibbons-26) Co-founder and CEO Sheldrick responded that "Whilst the overwhelming proportion of similar-sounding three-word combinations will be so far apart that an error is obvious, there will still be cases where similar sounding word combinations are nearby."[[31]](#cite_note-Wakefield-BBC-31) Further analysis by Tierney shows that in the London area, around 1 in 24 addresses will be confusable with another London address.[[9]](#cite_note-Tierney-9)

In September 2022, the [Department for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Culture, Media and Sport") used What3words to direct mourners to the end of [the queue](/wiki/Queue_for_the_lying-in-state_of_Elizabeth_II "Queue for the lying-in-state of Elizabeth II") to view the Queen lying in state in London. Of the first five codes published, four led to the wrong place,[[32]](#cite_note-Telegraph20220914-32) including [a suburb of London](/wiki/New_Denham "New Denham") some 15 miles (24 km) from the real end of the queue.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Officials later moved to an automated system to generate the identifiers, as they realised having people involved in the process resulted in typos.[[32]](#cite_note-Telegraph20220914-32)

A paper published in 2023[[8]](#cite_note-arthurplos-8) investigated the patented algorithm without using What3words's own wordlist. It found that using [linear congruence](/wiki/Linear_congruential_generator "Linear congruential generator") for address assignment does a poor job of randomising the wordlist. It also noted that the AutoSuggest feature did not return sufficient results to disambiguate an address. It concluded that "W3W should not be adopted as critical infrastructure without a thorough evaluation against a number of competing alternatives".[[8]](#cite_note-arthurplos-8)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

According to [Rory Sutherland](/wiki/Rory_Sutherland_(advertising_executive) "Rory Sutherland (advertising executive)") from the advertising agency [Ogilvy](/wiki/Ogilvy_(agency) "Ogilvy (agency)") in a 2014 op-ed piece for *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")*, the system's advantages are memorability, accuracy, and non-ambiguity in speech.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Mountain rescue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mountain rescue")]

Mountain rescue services in the UK have warned against relying on the app:

* In December 2019, the [Lake District Search & Mountain Rescue Association](/wiki/Mountain_rescue_in_England_and_Wales#Lake_District "Mountain rescue in England and Wales") noted that "mishearing or misspelling words tended to cause problems" and warned hikers not to rely on it.[[10]](#cite_note-Times20191226-10)
* In June 2021, [Mountain Rescue England and Wales](/wiki/Mountain_Rescue_England_and_Wales "Mountain Rescue England and Wales") raised concerns about the credibility of reported What3words coordinates, following incorrect information being given about 45 locations over 12 months. Spelling issues and local accents were reported as being part of the problem.[[35]](#cite_note-BBC20210601-35)
* In December 2023, mountain rescue teams from [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire") and the [Durham Dales](/wiki/Durham_Dales "Durham Dales") warned that the app led them to locations 8 and 10 km (5 and 6 mi) away from the correct spots.[[36]](#cite_note-SA20231209-36)

### Other emergency services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other emergency services")]

Since 2019, What3words has seen adoption by police, fire and ambulance services, who can use it for free[[37]](#cite_note-smh2020-37) and participate in media campaigns provided by What3Words[[38]](#cite_note-wdtk2022-38) to promote the app.[[39]](#cite_note-warks2022-39)[[40]](#cite_note-haldimand2022-40)[[41]](#cite_note-notts2022-41) By September 2021, more than 85 percent of British emergency services teams used What3words, including the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") and [London Fire Brigade](/wiki/London_Fire_Brigade "London Fire Brigade").[[42]](#cite_note-FinTimes-42)[[7]](#cite_note-bbc2019-7) Support has also been added to the Australian Government's [Triple Zero](/wiki/000_(emergency_telephone_number) "000 (emergency telephone number)") Emergency Plus App.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

* In September 2019, the [Scottish Ambulance Service](/wiki/Scottish_Ambulance_Service "Scottish Ambulance Service") used the app to share the location of an injured hillwalker with the [coastguard](/wiki/HM_Coastguard "HM Coastguard").[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* In February 2020, [Ambulance Tasmania](/wiki/Ambulance_Tasmania "Ambulance Tasmania") sent a link to locate an injured bushwalker.[[37]](#cite_note-smh2020-37)
* In October 2020, the [Singapore Police Force](/wiki/Singapore_Police_Force "Singapore Police Force") asked two lost 14-year-old boys to download and use the app.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* In July 2022, [Baldwin County, Alabama](/wiki/Baldwin_County,_Alabama "Baldwin County, Alabama") dispatchers used the app to pinpoint a capsized kayaker, with responders saying that they "were within 50 yards (45 m) and couldn't see him because of the conditions in the water".[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* In August 2022, Tröstau [Fire Brigade](/wiki/German_Fire_Services "German Fire Services") used the app to communicate a location to the Wunsiedel [mountain rescue service](/wiki/Bergwacht "Bergwacht").[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* In September 2022, [Halton](/wiki/Regional_Municipality_of_Halton "Regional Municipality of Halton") emergency services in Canada located an injured climber using the app.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* In August 2022, [East of England Ambulance Service](/wiki/East_of_England_Ambulance_Service "East of England Ambulance Service") took "nearly 10 minutes" to identify a cycle path after being given a What3words address. When approached by [Cambridge News](/wiki/Cambridge_News "Cambridge News"), the Ambulance Service continued to recommend the app, and did not respond to a query about why they were unable to quickly pinpoint the precise location using the system.[[49]](#cite_note-CN20220813-49)
* In October 2025, first responders were able to locate an injured hunter outside of [Ridgeway, Wisconsin](/wiki/Ridgeway,_Wisconsin "Ridgeway, Wisconsin") within 25 minutes thanks to the app.[[50]](#cite_note-s989-50)

### Proprietary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Proprietary")]

The What3words system has been criticised for being controlled by a private business, and the software for being patented and not freely usable.[[7]](#cite_note-bbc2019-7)

The company has pursued a policy of issuing copyright claims against individuals and organisations that have hosted or published files of the What3words [algorithm](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") or [reverse-engineered](/wiki/Reverse_engineering "Reverse engineering") code that replicates the service's functionality, such as the [free](/wiki/Free_software "Free software") and [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") implementation WhatFreeWords; the whatfreewords.org website was taken down following a [Digital Millennium Copyright Act](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act "Digital Millennium Copyright Act") (DMCA) [take-down notice](/wiki/Notice_and_take_down "Notice and take down") issued by What3words.[[51]](#cite_note-51) This policy has extended to removing comments on social media which refer to unauthorised versions. In late April 2021, a security researcher who had offered on [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") to share WhatFreeWords software was contacted by What3Words's law firm, requiring him to delete the tweets and the software, and implying that legal action might follow non-compliance.[[52]](#cite_note-techcrunch210430-52)

### Parody

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Parody")]

The site has been parodied by others who have created services including What3Emojis[[53]](#cite_note-53) using [emojis](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji"), What3Birds[[54]](#cite_note-54) using [British birds](/wiki/List_of_birds_of_Great_Britain "List of birds of Great Britain"), What3fucks[[55]](#cite_note-55) using [swear words](/wiki/Profanity "Profanity"), Four King Maps[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) also using swear words (covering only the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles")), and What3Numbers[[58]](#cite_note-58) using [OpenStreetMap](/wiki/OpenStreetMap "OpenStreetMap") tile identifiers.

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Awards")]

* Grand Prix for Innovation at the 2015 [Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity")[[59]](#cite_note-59)
* [The Tech Awards](/wiki/The_Tech_Awards "The Tech Awards") 2015 [Sobrato Organization](/wiki/John_Sobrato "John Sobrato") Economic Development Award[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=What3words&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Geocode](/wiki/Geocode "Geocode") – Code that represents a geographic entity (location or object)
  + [Geohash](/wiki/Geohash "Geohash") – Public domain geocoding invented in 2008
  + [Mapcode](/wiki/Mapcode "Mapcode") – Open-source geocode system
  + [Maidenhead Locator System](/wiki/Maidenhead_Locator_System "Maidenhead Locator System") – Geocode system used by radio amateurs
  + [Open Location Code](/wiki/Open_Location_Code "Open Location Code") – Short text encoding of geographic coordinates
* [Advanced Mobile Location](/wiki/Advanced_Mobile_Location "Advanced Mobile Location") – Widely implemented caller location for emergency services
* [Geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") – System to specify locations on Earth
  + [Projected coordinate system](/wiki/Projected_coordinate_system "Projected coordinate system") – Cartesian geographic coordinate system
  + [Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator_coordinate_system "Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system") – Map projection system
